topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical needles pricking my skin. I stood at the edge of the room, my breath catching in my throat as I took in the canvases. Each brushstroke, a piece of my quiet, internal landscape, hung there, illuminated under harsh spotlights I hadn't asked for. A familiar tightness began to coil in my chest, a dull ache that made my shoulders hunch inward. I watched a patron point, their voice loud and booming, dissecting the texture of a piece I’d poured weeks of my solitude into. My hands, clasped tightly behind my back, started to tremble almost imperceptibly. I wanted to melt into the polished floor, to become invisible, to erase the vibrant colors that felt suddenly exposed, judged, and utterly unwelcome.

[story 2]

"This piece is truly arresting," a curator murmured, circling a large abstract painting as if it were a rare jewel. The sound of his approval, so detached and professional, scraped against the fragile composure I was desperately trying to maintain. I kept my gaze fixed on the doorway, focusing on the pattern of the expensive rug beneath my worn boots. A strange heat bloomed low in my stomach, a churning discomfort that made swallowing feel like a monumental effort. When someone approached me, offering a polite but invasive compliment about my 'unique vision,' my reply came out thin and reedy. I managed a small, tight smile, careful not to let my eyes betray the sudden, sharp need to retreat entirely.

[story 3]

The air in the exhibition space was thick, heavy with the scent of varnish and expectation. I found myself pacing the perimeter, a restless energy buzzing just beneath my skin. Every glance directed my way felt like an interrogation. When a group of admirers clustered near my charcoal sketches, their hushed commentary washed over me, a tide of unsolicited interpretation. I felt a distinct pressure building behind my eyes, a desperate urge to shield myself from the scrutiny. I stopped near a darkened alcove, leaning against the cool wall, focusing intently on the minute grain of the plaster, trying to anchor myself against the overwhelming feeling of being utterly seen, completely uninvited.
